1	SHELL :=/bin/bash
2	
3	all: build
4	.PHONY: all
5	
6	# Include the library makefile
7	include $(addprefix ./vendor/github.com/openshift/build-machinery-go/make/, \
8		golang.mk \
9		targets/openshift/deps.mk \
10		targets/openshift/images.mk \
11		targets/openshift/yaml-patch.mk\
12		lib/tmp.mk\
13	)
14	
15	# Include the integration/e2e setup makefile.
16	include ./test/integration-test.mk
17	include ./test/e2e-test.mk
18	include ./test/olm-test.mk
19	include ./test/kind-images.mk
20	
21	OPERATOR_SDK?=$(PERMANENT_TMP_GOPATH)/bin/operator-sdk
22	OPERATOR_SDK_VERSION?=v1.32.0
23	operatorsdk_gen_dir:=$(dir $(OPERATOR_SDK))
24	
25	HELM?=$(PERMANENT_TMP_GOPATH)/bin/helm
26	HELM_VERSION?=v3.14.0
27	helm_gen_dir:=$(dir $(HELM))
28	
29	# RELEASED_CSV_VERSION indicates the last released operator version.
30	# can find the released operator version from
31	# https://github.com/k8s-operatorhub/community-operators/tree/main/operators/cluster-manager
32	# https://github.com/k8s-operatorhub/community-operators/tree/main/operators/klusterlet
33	RELEASED_CSV_VERSION?=0.14.0
34	export RELEASED_CSV_VERSION
35	
36	# CSV_VERSION is used to generate latest CSV manifests
37	CSV_VERSION?=9.9.9
38	export CSV_VERSION
39	
40	OPERATOR_SDK_ARCHOS:=linux_amd64
41	HELM_ARCHOS:=linux-amd64
42	ifeq ($(GOHOSTOS),darwin)
43		ifeq ($(GOHOSTARCH),amd64)
44			OPERATOR_SDK_ARCHOS:=darwin_amd64
45			HELM_ARCHOS:=darwin-amd64
46		endif
47		ifeq ($(GOHOSTARCH),arm64)
48			OPERATOR_SDK_ARCHOS:=darwin_arm64
49			HELM_ARCHOS:=darwin-arm64
50		endif
51	endif
52	
53	# Add packages to do unit test
54	GO_TEST_PACKAGES :=./pkg/...
55	GO_TEST_FLAGS := -race -coverprofile=coverage.out
56	
57	IMAGE_REGISTRY?=quay.io/open-cluster-management
58	IMAGE_TAG?=latest
59	
60	OPERATOR_IMAGE_NAME ?= $(IMAGE_REGISTRY)/registration-operator:$(IMAGE_TAG)
61	# WORK_IMAGE can be set in the env to override calculated value
62	WORK_IMAGE ?= $(IMAGE_REGISTRY)/work:$(IMAGE_TAG)
63	# REGISTRATION_IMAGE can be set in the env to override calculated value
64	REGISTRATION_IMAGE ?= $(IMAGE_REGISTRY)/registration:$(IMAGE_TAG)
65	# PLACEMENT_IMAGE can be set in the env to override calculated value
66	PLACEMENT_IMAGE ?= $(IMAGE_REGISTRY)/placement:$(IMAGE_TAG)
67	# ADDON_MANAGER_IMAGE can be set in the env to override calculated value
68	ADDON_MANAGER_IMAGE ?= $(IMAGE_REGISTRY)/addon-manager:$(IMAGE_TAG)
69	
70	$(call build-image,registration,$(REGISTRATION_IMAGE),./build/Dockerfile.registration,.)
71	$(call build-image,work,$(WORK_IMAGE),./build/Dockerfile.work,.)
72	$(call build-image,placement,$(PLACEMENT_IMAGE),./build/Dockerfile.placement,.)
73	$(call build-image,registration-operator,$(OPERATOR_IMAGE_NAME),./build/Dockerfile.registration-operator,.)
74	$(call build-image,addon-manager,$(ADDON_MANAGER_IMAGE),./build/Dockerfile.addon,.)
75	
76	copy-crd: ensure-yaml-patch
77		bash -x hack/copy-crds.sh $(YAML_PATCH)
78	
79	update: copy-crd update-csv
80	
81	test-unit: envtest-setup
82	
83	update-csv: ensure-operator-sdk ensure-helm
84		bash -x hack/update-csv.sh
85	
86	verify-crds: ensure-yaml-patch
87		bash -x hack/verify-crds.sh $(YAML_PATCH)
88	
89	.PHONY: lint
90	lint:
91		@bash -o pipefail -c 'curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/open-cluster-management-io/sdk-go/main/ci/lint/run-lint.sh | bash'
92	
93	install-golang-gci:
94		go install github.com/daixiang0/gci@v0.13.7
95	
96	fmt-imports: install-golang-gci
97		gci write --skip-generated -s standard -s default -s "prefix(open-cluster-management.io)" -s localmodule cmd pkg test dependencymagnet
98	
99	verify-fmt-imports: install-golang-gci
100		@output=$$(gci diff --skip-generated -s standard -s default -s "prefix(open-cluster-management.io)" -s localmodule cmd pkg test dependencymagnet); \
101		if [ -n "$$output" ]; then \
102		    echo "Diff output is not empty: $$output"; \
103		    echo "Please run 'make fmt-imports' to format the golang files imports automatically."; \
104		    exit 1; \
105		else \
106		    echo "Diff output is empty"; \
107		fi
108	
109	verify: verify-fmt-imports verify-crds lint
110	
111	ensure-operator-sdk:
112	ifeq "" "$(wildcard $(OPERATOR_SDK))"
113		$(info Installing operator-sdk into '$(OPERATOR_SDK)')
114		mkdir -p '$(operatorsdk_gen_dir)'
115		curl -s -f -L https://github.com/operator-framework/operator-sdk/releases/download/$(OPERATOR_SDK_VERSION)/operator-sdk_$(OPERATOR_SDK_ARCHOS) -o '$(OPERATOR_SDK)'
116		chmod +x '$(OPERATOR_SDK)';
117	else
118		$(info Using existing operator-sdk from "$(OPERATOR_SDK)")
119	endif
120	
121	ensure-helm:
122	ifeq "" "$(wildcard $(HELM))"
123		$(info Installing helm into '$(HELM)')
124		mkdir -p '$(helm_gen_dir)'
125		curl -s -f -L https://get.helm.sh/helm-$(HELM_VERSION)-$(HELM_ARCHOS).tar.gz -o '$(helm_gen_dir)$(HELM_VERSION)-$(HELM_ARCHOS).tar.gz'
126		tar -zvxf '$(helm_gen_dir)/$(HELM_VERSION)-$(HELM_ARCHOS).tar.gz' -C $(helm_gen_dir)
127		mv $(helm_gen_dir)/$(HELM_ARCHOS)/helm $(HELM)
128		rm -rf $(helm_gen_dir)/$(HELM_ARCHOS)
129		chmod +x '$(HELM)';
130	else
131		$(info Using existing helm from "$(HELM)")
132	endif
133	
134

1	![OCM logo](assets/ocm-logo.png)
2	
3	[![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-Apache_2.0-blue.svg)](https://opensource.org/licenses/Apache-2.0)
4	[![CII Best Practices](https://bestpractices.coreinfrastructure.org/projects/5376/badge)](https://bestpractices.coreinfrastructure.org/projects/5376)
5	[![OpenSSF Scorecard](https://api.securityscorecards.dev/projects/github.com/open-cluster-management-io/ocm/badge)](https://api.securityscorecards.dev/projects/github.com/open-cluster-management-io/ocm)
6	[![FOSSA Status](https://app.fossa.com/api/projects/git%2Bgithub.com%2Fopen-cluster-management-io%2Focm.svg?type=shield&issueType=license)](https://app.fossa.com/projects/git%2Bgithub.com%2Fopen-cluster-management-io%2Focm?ref=badge_shield&issueType=license)
7	[![Artifact HUB Cluster Manager](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https://artifacthub.io/badge/repository/cluster-manager)](https://artifacthub.io/packages/olm/community-operators/cluster-manager)
8	[![Artifact HUB Klusterlet](https://img.shields.io/endpoint?url=https://artifacthub.io/badge/repository/klusterlet)](https://artifacthub.io/packages/olm/community-operators/klusterlet)
9	
10	Open Cluster Management (OCM) is a [Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) sandbox project](https://www.cncf.io/projects/open-cluster-management) focused on multicluster and multicloud scenarios for Kubernetes applications. At its core, OCM exists to make running, managing, and utilizing heterogeneous, multicluster environments simpler.
11	
12	![CNCF logo](assets/cncf.png)
13	
14	## Project Goals and Objectives
15	
16	We believe that no one runs just one cluster, and making multicluster management as easy as possible encourages community growth across all projects, empowering our users and their customers. Our core goal is to "multicluster everything."
17	
18	OCM provides a flexible, extensible, and open model that allows any software project to easily understand how to run in a multicluster way. We align with and contribute to the SIG-Multicluster APIs for multicluster management while developing and sharing key concepts and components that make it easier and more accessible for any project, large or small, to "do multicluster."
19	
20	### Differentiation in the Cloud Native Landscape
21	
22	Open Cluster Management differentiates itself in several key ways:
23	
24	- **Vendor Neutral**: Avoid vendor lock-in by using APIs that are not tied to any cloud providers or proprietary platforms
25	- **Standards-Based**: We provide both a reference implementation of the APIs put forward by SIG-Multicluster as well as extended capabilities to accentuate them for numerous additional use cases
26	- **Plug-and-Play Architecture**: By standardizing on a single PlacementDecision API, any scheduler can publish its choices and any consumer can subscribe to them, eliminating friction and enabling true interoperability
27	- **Extensible Framework**: Our add-on structure allows projects to gain the benefits of simple, secure, and vendor-neutral multicloud management with minimal code changes
28	
29	## What OCM Does
30	
31	OCM utilizes open APIs and an easy-to-use add-on structure to provide comprehensive multicluster management capabilities. We do this because we believe that managing multiple clusters should be simple and easy. With the variety of both free and proprietary cloud and on-premises offerings, it is important to help the community utilize these heterogeneous environments to their fullest potential.
32	
33	Our architecture is modular and extensible, built to always model Kubernetes principles. We utilize open declarative APIs and build code with DevOps and automation. OCM runs on a hub-spoke model that can survive failure and recovery of components without interrupting workloads. It's containerized, vendor-neutral, and focuses on declarative management style aligned with GitOps principles.
34	
35	### Core Architecture
36	
37	The OCM architecture uses a hub-spoke model where the hub centralizes control of all managed clusters. An agent, called the klusterlet, resides on each managed cluster to manage registration to the hub and run instructions from the hub.
38	
39	![OCM Architecture](assets/ocm-arch.png)
40	
41	## Core Components
42	
43	### Cluster Inventory
44	
45	Registration of multiple clusters to a hub cluster to place them for management. For comprehensive details on cluster inventory management, see the [cluster inventory documentation](https://open-cluster-management.io/docs/concepts/cluster-inventory/). OCM implements a secure "double opt-in handshaking" protocol that requires explicit consent from both hub cluster administrators and managed cluster administrators to establish the connection. 
46	
47	The registration process creates an mTLS connection between the registration-agent on the managed cluster (Klusterlet) and the registration-controller on the hub (Cluster Manager). Once registered, each managed cluster is assigned a dedicated namespace on the hub for isolation and security. The registration-controller continuously monitors cluster health and manages cluster lifecycle operations including certificate rotation and permission management.
48	
49	### Work Distribution
50	
51	The [ManifestWork API](https://open-cluster-management.io/docs/concepts/work-distribution/) enables resources to be applied to managed clusters from a hub cluster. ManifestWork acts as a wrapper containing Kubernetes resource manifests that need to be distributed and applied to specific managed clusters.
52	
53	The work-agent running on each managed cluster actively pulls ManifestWork resources from its dedicated namespace on the hub and applies them locally. This pull-based approach ensures scalability and eliminates the need to store managed cluster credentials on the hub. The API supports sophisticated features including update strategies, conflict resolution, resource ordering, and status feedback to provide comprehensive resource distribution capabilities.
54	
55	### Content Placement
56	
57	Dynamic placement of content and behavior across multiple clusters. The [Placement API](https://open-cluster-management.io/docs/concepts/content-placement/placement/) is a sophisticated scheduler for multicluster environments that operates on a Hub-Spoke model and is deeply integrated with the Work API.
58	
59	The Placement API allows administrators to use vendor-neutral selectors to dynamically choose clusters based on labels, resource capacity, or custom criteria. The PlacementDecision resource provides a list of selected clusters that can be consumed by any scheduler or controller.
60	
61	### Add-on Framework
62	
63	[OCM Add-ons](https://open-cluster-management.io/docs/concepts/add-on-extensibility/addon/) provide a clear framework that allows developers to easily add their software to OCM and make it multicluster aware. Add-ons are simple to write and are fully documented and maintained according to specifications in the [addon-framework](https://github.com/open-cluster-management-io/addon-framework) repository.
64	
65	The framework handles the complex aspects of multicluster deployment including registration, lifecycle management, configuration distribution, and status reporting. Add-ons are used by multiple projects such as Argo, Submariner, KubeVela, and more. They are also used extensively for internal components of OCM, ensuring the framework is actively developed and maintained. With add-ons, any project can plug into OCM with minimal code and almost instantaneously become multicluster aware.
66	
67	## Built-in Extensions
68	
69	These optional but valuable capabilities extend OCM's core functionality for specific use cases.
70	
71	### Cluster Lifecycle Management
72	
73	OCM integrates seamlessly with [Cluster API](https://cluster-api.sigs.k8s.io/) to provide comprehensive cluster lifecycle management. Cluster API is a Kubernetes sub-project focused on providing declarative APIs and tooling to simplify provisioning, upgrading, and operating multiple Kubernetes clusters.
74	
75	The integration allows OCM to work alongside Cluster API management planes, where clusters provisioned through Cluster API can be automatically registered with OCM for multicluster management. OCM's hub cluster can run in the same cluster as the Cluster API management plane, enabling seamless workflows from cluster creation to workload deployment and policy enforcement. See the [Cluster API integration guide](solutions/cluster-api/) for detailed setup instructions.
76	
77	### Application Lifecycle Management
78	
79	Leverage the [Argo CD](https://argo-cd.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) add-on for OCM to enable decentralized, pull-based application deployment to managed clusters. The OCM Argo CD add-on uses a hub-spoke architecture to deliver Argo CD Applications from the OCM hub cluster to registered managed clusters.
80	
81	Unlike traditional push-based deployment models, this pull mechanism provides several advantages:
82	- **Scalability**: Hub-spoke pattern offers better scalability
83	- **Security**: Cluster credentials don't have to be stored in a centralized environment
84	- **Resilience**: Reduces the impact of a single point of centralized failure
85	
86	See [Argo CD OCM add-on](solutions/deploy-argocd-apps-pull/) for details on installing the add-on and deploying applications across multiple clusters.
87	
88	### Governance, Risk, and Compliance (GRC)
89	
90	Use prebuilt security and configuration controllers to enforce policies on Kubernetes configuration across your clusters. Policy controllers allow the declarative expression of desired conditions that can be audited or enforced against a set of managed clusters.
91	
92	Related repositories:
93	- [config-policy-controller](https://github.com/open-cluster-management-io/config-policy-controller)
94	- [governance-policy-framework-addon](https://github.com/open-cluster-management-io/governance-policy-framework-addon)
95	- [governance-policy-propagator](https://github.com/open-cluster-management-io/governance-policy-propagator)
96	
97	## Cloud Native Use Cases
98	
99	Everything OCM does is Cloud Native by definition. Our comprehensive [User Scenarios](https://open-cluster-management.io/docs/scenarios/) section provides detailed examples of how OCM enables various multicluster and multicloud use cases.
100	
101	## Getting Started
102	
103	You can use the [clusteradm CLI](https://github.com/open-cluster-management-io/clusteradm) to bootstrap a control plane for multicluster management. To set up a multicluster environment with OCM enabled on your local machine, follow the instructions in [setup dev environment](solutions/setup-dev-environment).
104	
105	For developers looking to contribute to OCM, see our comprehensive [Development Guide](development.md) which covers development environment setup, code standards, testing, and contribution workflows.
106	
107	## External Integrations
108	
109	We constantly work with other open-source projects to make multicluster management easier:
110	
111	- **[Argo CD](https://argo-cd.readthedocs.io/en/stable/operator-manual/applicationset/Generators-Cluster-Decision-Resource/#how-it-works)** (CNCF): OCM supplies Argo CD with ClusterDecision resources via Argo CD's Cluster Decision Resource Generator, enabling it to select target clusters for GitOps deployments
112	- **[Clusternet](https://github.com/clusternet/clusternet)**: (CNCF) Multicluster orchestration that can easily plug into OCM with [clusternet addon](https://github.com/open-cluster-management-io/addon-contrib/tree/main/clusternet-addon)
113	- **[KubeVela](https://kubevela.io/docs/platform-engineers/system-operation/working-with-ocm/)** (CNCF): KubeVela develops an integration addon to work with OCM supporting application deployment across multiple clusters
114	- **[KubeStellar](https://docs.kubestellar.io/latest/direct/start-from-ocm/)** (CNCF): KubeStellar uses OCM as the backend of multicluster management
115	- **[Kueue](https://kueue.sigs.k8s.io/docs/tasks/manage/setup_multikueue/#optional-setup-multikueue-with-open-cluster-management)** (Kubernetes SIGs): OCM supplies Kueue with a streamlined MultiKueue setup process, automated generation of MultiKueue specific Kubeconfig, and enhanced multicluster scheduling capabilities
116	- **[Submariner](https://submariner.io/)**: (CNCF) Provides multicluster networking connectivity with automated deployment and management
117	
118	## Documentation and Resources
119	
120	For comprehensive information about OCM, visit our [website](https://open-cluster-management.io/) with detailed sections on [key concepts](https://open-cluster-management.io/docs/concepts/).
121	
122	## Community
123	
124	Connect with the OCM community:
125	
126	- [Website](https://open-cluster-management.io)
127	- [Slack](https://kubernetes.slack.com/channels/open-cluster-mgmt)
128	- [Mailing group](https://groups.google.com/g/open-cluster-management)
129	- [Community meetings](https://calendar.google.com/calendar/u/0/embed?src=openclustermanagement@gmail.com)
130	- [YouTube channel](https://www.youtube.com/c/OpenClusterManagement)
131	
132	## License
133	
134	This project is licensed under the Apache License 2.0 - see the [LICENSE](LICENSE) file for details.
135

95		@# Step 1: Delete ClusterManager CR and wait for controller to clean up

content.content:
1	# Development Guide
2	
3	This document provides comprehensive guidelines for new contributors developing the Open Cluster Management (OCM) project. It also serves as a reference for AI tools performing code reviews and analysis.
4	
5	## Table of Contents
6	
7	- [Project Overview](#project-overview)
8	- [Development Environment Setup](#development-environment-setup)
9	- [Code Organization](#code-organization)
10	- [Development Workflow](#development-workflow)
11	- [Code Standards and Style](#code-standards-and-style)
12	- [Testing Guidelines](#testing-guidelines)
13	- [API Development](#api-development)
14	- [Build and Release](#build-and-release)
15	- [Troubleshooting](#troubleshooting)
16	
17	## Project Overview
18	
19	Open Cluster Management (OCM) is a CNCF sandbox project that provides multicluster and multicloud management capabilities for Kubernetes. The project consists of five core components:
20	
21	- **registration**: Manages cluster registration and certificate lifecycle
22	- **placement**: Handles content placement decisions across clusters
23	- **work**: Distributes and applies manifests to managed clusters
24	- **registration-operator**: Deploys and manages OCM components
25	- **addon-manager**: Manages OCM add-ons lifecycle
26	
27	### Key Technologies
28	
29	- **Language**: Go 1.25.0
30	- **Framework**: Kubernetes operators with controller-runtime
31	- **Build System**: Make with OpenShift build machinery
32	- **Testing**: Ginkgo/Gomega for BDD-style tests
33	- **Packaging**: Helm charts and OLM bundles
34	
35	## Development Environment Setup
36	
37	### Prerequisites
38	
39	- Go 1.25.0 ([installation guide](https://go.dev/doc/install))
40	- Docker or Podman (container engine)
41	- [Kind](https://kind.sigs.k8s.io/) (local Kubernetes clusters)
42	- [kubectl](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/tools/) (Kubernetes CLI)
43	- [clusteradm](https://github.com/open-cluster-management-io/clusteradm) (OCM CLI)
44	
45	### Quick Start
46	
47	1. **Create development cluster**:
48	   ```bash
49	   kind create cluster --name ocm-dev
50	   ```
51	
52	2. **Initialize OCM control plane**:
53	   ```bash
54	   # Replace <TAG> with a concrete release (e.g., vX.Y.Z)
55	   curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/open-cluster-management-io/clusteradm/<TAG>/hack/install.sh | bash
56	   clusteradm init --wait --bundle-version="<BUNDLE_VERSION>"
57	   ```
58	
59	3. **Join cluster as managed cluster**:
60	   ```bash
61	   clusteradm join --hub-token <token> --hub-apiserver <url> --wait --cluster-name cluster1 --force-internal-endpoint-lookup --bundle-version="<BUNDLE_VERSION>"
62	   clusteradm accept --clusters cluster1
63	   ```
64	
65	4. **Verify installation**:
66	   ```bash
67	   kubectl get clustermanager,managedcluster
68	   kubectl get pods -A | grep open-cluster-management
69	   ```
70	
71	## Code Organization
72	
73	```
74	ocm/
75	├── cmd/                    # Main applications entry points
76	│   ├── addon/             # Addon manager
77	│   ├── placement/         # Placement controller
78	│   ├── registration/      # Registration controller
79	│   ├── registration-operator/ # Operator
80	│   ├── server/            # GRPC server
81	│   └── work/              # Work controller
82	├── pkg/                   # Core business logic
83	│   ├── addon/             # Addon framework and controllers
84	│   ├── operator/          # Operator controllers
85	│   ├── placement/         # Placement logic and controllers
86	│   ├── registration/      # Registration logic
87	│   └── work/              # Work distribution logic
88	├── manifests/             # Deployment manifests
89	├── deploy/                # Helm charts and OLM bundles
90	├── test/                  # Test suites
91	│   ├── e2e/              # End-to-end tests
92	│   └── integration/      # Integration tests
93	└── solutions/             # Usage examples and solutions
94	```
95	
96	### Key Packages
97	
98	- `pkg/common/`: Shared utilities and helpers
99	- `pkg/registration/hub/`: Hub-side registration controllers
100	- `pkg/registration/spoke/`: Managed cluster registration logic

100	- `pkg/registration/spoke/`: Managed cluster registration logic
101	- `pkg/work/hub/`: Hub-side work distribution
102	- `pkg/work/spoke/`: Managed cluster work application
103	- `pkg/placement/controllers/`: Placement scheduling and decision logic
104	- `pkg/addon/controllers/`: Addon lifecycle management controllers
105	
106	## Development Workflow
107	
108	### 1. Making Code Changes
109	
110	```bash
111	# Fork and clone the repository
112	git clone https://github.com/<your-username>/ocm.git
113	cd ocm
114	
115	# Create feature branch
116	git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name
117	
118	# Make changes and commit with DCO sign-off
119	git commit -s -m "Add new feature: description"
120	```
121	
122	### 2. Building Images
123	
124	Build all images:
125	```bash
126	IMAGE_REGISTRY=quay.io/your-org IMAGE_TAG=dev make images
127	```
128	
129	Build specific component:
130	```bash
131	IMAGE_REGISTRY=quay.io/your-org IMAGE_TAG=dev make image-registration
132	```
133	
134	Load images to kind cluster:
135	```bash
136	kind load docker-image quay.io/your-org/registration:dev --name ocm-dev
137	```
138	
139	### 3. Testing Changes
140	
141	Update operator images:
142	```bash
143	kubectl set image -n open-cluster-management deployment/cluster-manager registration-operator=quay.io/your-org/registration-operator:dev
144	```
145	
146	Update component images via CustomResource:
147	```bash
148	kubectl edit clustermanager cluster-manager  # For hub components
149	kubectl edit klusterlet klusterlet           # For spoke components
150	```
151	
152	## Code Standards and Style
153	
154	### Go Code Style
155	
156	- **Follow standard Go conventions**: Use `gofmt`, `go vet`, and `golangci-lint`
157	- **Variable naming**: Use camelCase for local variables, PascalCase for exported items
158	- **Comments**: Write clear comments for exported functions and complex logic
159	- **Interface design**: Keep interfaces small and focused (interface segregation principle)
160	
161	### Import Organization
162	
163	Imports must be organized in the following order:
164	1. Standard library packages
165	2. Third-party packages  
166	3. OCM packages
167	
168	```go
169	import (
170	    // Standard library
171	    "context"
172	    "fmt"
173	    "time"
174	
175	    // Third-party
176	    "github.com/spf13/cobra"
177	    "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/api/errors"
178	    "sigs.k8s.io/controller-runtime/pkg/client"
179	
180	    // OCM packages
181	    "open-cluster-management.io/api/cluster/v1"
182	    "open-cluster-management.io/ocm/pkg/common/helpers"
183	)
184	```
185	
186	### Code Quality Requirements
187	
188	All code must pass these checks:
189	```bash
190	make verify                      # Run all verification checks
191	make verify-gocilint            # Linting with golangci-lint
192	make verify-fmt-imports         # Import formatting verification
193	make verify-crds               # CRD consistency checks
194	```
195	
196	Auto-format imports:
197	```bash
198	make fmt-imports                # Automatically format import statements
199	```
200	
201	### Naming Conventions
202	
203	- **Files**: Use snake_case (e.g., `cluster_controller.go`)
204	- **Packages**: Use lowercase, single words when possible
205	- **Constants**: Use CamelCase (PascalCase for exported constants); reserve ALL_CAPS only when mirroring external specs
206	- **Controllers**: Suffix with `Controller` (e.g., `PlacementController`)
207	- **Interfaces**: Use descriptive names, often ending with `-er` (e.g., `ClusterLister`)
208	
209	### Documentation Standards
210	
211	- All exported functions, types, and constants must have comments
212	- Comments should start with the name of the item being documented
213	- Use complete sentences and proper punctuation
214	- Include examples for complex APIs
215	
216	## Testing Guidelines
217	
218	### Unit Tests
219	
220	```bash
221	make test-unit                    # Run all unit tests
222	go test ./pkg/registration/...    # Test specific package
223	```
224	
225	### Integration Tests
226	
227	Integration tests use controller-runtime's envtest framework:
228	
229	```bash
230	make test-integration                      # All integration tests
231	make test-registration-integration         # Registration only
232	make test-placement-integration           # Placement only
233	make test-work-integration               # Work only
234	make test-addon-integration              # Addon only
235	```
236	
237	### E2E Tests
238	
239	End-to-end tests run against real clusters and require a kind cluster with proper setup:
240	
241	```bash
242	# 1. Create kind cluster and set KUBECONFIG
243	kind get kubeconfig --name ocm-e2e > /tmp/kubeconfig-ocm-e2e
244	export KUBECONFIG=/tmp/kubeconfig-ocm-e2e
245	
246	# 2. Run E2E tests with required variables
247	IMAGE_TAG=e2e KLUSTERLET_DEPLOY_MODE=Singleton make test-e2e
248	```
249	
250	E2E test variables:
251	- `IMAGE_TAG`: Tag for container images (default: latest)
252	- `KLUSTERLET_DEPLOY_MODE`: Deployment mode (Default, Singleton, SingletonHosted)
253	- `MANAGED_CLUSTER_NAME`: Name of the managed cluster (default: cluster1)
254	- `KUBECONFIG`: Must be set to point to test cluster
255	
256	### Test Organization
257	
258	- **Unit tests**: `*_test.go` files alongside source code
259	- **Integration tests**: `test/integration/` directory
260	- **E2E tests**: `test/e2e/` directory
261	- **Test utilities**: `test/framework/` and `pkg/*/testing/`
262	
263	### Test Writing Guidelines
264	
265	- Use Ginkgo/Gomega for BDD-style tests
266	- Write descriptive test names using `Describe`, `Context`, and `It`
267	- Use table-driven tests for multiple scenarios
268	- Mock external dependencies appropriately
269	- Ensure tests are deterministic and can run in parallel
270	
271	## API Development
272	
273	### Modifying APIs
274	
275	OCM APIs are defined in the separate [api repository](https://github.com/open-cluster-management-io/api). To modify APIs:
276	
277	1. **Clone both repositories**:
278	   ```bash
279	   git clone https://github.com/<your-username>/api.git
280	   git clone https://github.com/<your-username>/ocm.git
281	   ```
282	
283	2. **Make API changes** in the api repository and run:
284	   ```bash
285	   cd api
286	   make update  # Generates CRDs and DeepCopy methods
287	   ```
288	
289	3. **Use local API changes** in ocm repository:
290	   ```bash
291	   cd ocm
292	   go mod edit -replace open-cluster-management.io/api=/path/to/local/api
293	   go mod tidy && go mod vendor
294	   make update  # Updates CRDs, Helm charts, and operator manifests
295	   ```
296	
297	4. **Remove replace directive** before submitting PR
298

306	
307	## Build and Release
308	
309	### Build System
310	
311	The project uses OpenShift's build-machinery-go:
312	
313	```bash
314	make all                        # Build all binaries
315	make images                     # Build all container images
316	make update                     # Update all generated manifests
317	```
318	
319	### Image Build
320	
321	Images are built for multiple architectures:
322	- `linux/amd64`
323	- `linux/arm64`
324	
325	Registry configuration:
326	```bash
327	export IMAGE_REGISTRY=quay.io/open-cluster-management
328	export IMAGE_TAG=v0.x.x
329	```
330	
331	### Available Make Targets
332	
333	```bash
334	make build                      # Build binaries
335	make images                     # Build container images  
336	make test-unit                  # Run unit tests
337	make test-integration          # Run integration tests
338	make test-e2e                  # Run E2E tests
339	make verify                    # Run all verification checks
340	make update                    # Update generated files
341	make clean                     # Clean build artifacts
342	```
343	
344	## Troubleshooting
345	
346	### Common Issues
347	
348	1. **API Server Connection Issues**:
349	   ```bash
350	   kubectl cluster-info          # Verify cluster connectivity
351	   kubectl get nodes            # Check cluster health
352	   ```
353	
354	2. **Image Pull Failures**:
355	   ```bash
356	   kind load docker-image <image>  # Load to kind cluster
357	   kubectl describe pod <pod>      # Check image pull status
358	   ```
359	
360	3. **Certificate Issues**:
361	   ```bash
362	   kubectl get csr               # Check certificate requests
363	   kubectl get secrets -A | grep tls  # Check TLS secrets
364	   ```
365	
366	4. **Operator Issues**:
367	   ```bash
368	   kubectl logs -n open-cluster-management deployment/cluster-manager
369	   kubectl get clustermanager cluster-manager -o yaml
370	   ```
371	
372	### Debug Tools
373	
374	- Enable debug logging: Set `--v=4` or higher in component flags
375	- Use `kubectl describe` for resource status
376	- Check controller logs in `open-cluster-management*` namespaces
377	- Verify RBAC permissions with `kubectl auth can-i`
378	
379	### Performance Debugging
380	
381	- Use `go tool pprof` for CPU/memory profiling
382	- Monitor metrics endpoints (when enabled)
383	- Check resource utilization with `kubectl top`
384	
385	## Contributing Guidelines
386	
387	1. **Submit an issue** describing proposed changes
388	2. **Fork and develop** following this guide
389	3. **Add tests** for new functionality
390	4. **Run quality checks**: `make verify test-unit`

392	6. **Address review feedback** promptly
393	
394	For detailed contribution guidelines, see [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md).

### 4. ARCHITECTURE

**What This Project Does**:
OCM is a CNCF sandbox project for multicluster and multicloud Kubernetes management. It provides a hub-spoke architecture where a central hub cluster manages multiple spoke (managed) clusters.

**Five Core Components** (as per development.md):

1. **registration** (`cmd/registration/main.go`)
   - Manages cluster registration and certificate lifecycle
   - Runs on both hub and spoke clusters
   - Hub-side: Controllers for registration API
   - Spoke-side: Registration agent
   - Binary: `registration`

2. **work** (`cmd/work/main.go`)
   - Distributes and applies ManifestWork to managed clusters
   - Hub-side: Work distribution controller
   - Spoke-side: Work agent (pull-based)
   - Binary: `work`

3. **placement** (`cmd/placement/main.go`)
   - Handles content placement decisions across clusters
   - Placement scheduler and PlacementDecision logic
   - Hub-side only
   - Binary: `placement`

4. **addon-manager** (`cmd/addon/main.go`)
   - Manages OCM add-on lifecycle
   - Hub-side only
   - Binary: `addon`

5. **registration-operator** (`cmd/registration-operator/main.go`)
   - Deploys and manages OCM components
   - Operator pattern using controller-runtime
   - Binary: `registration-operator`
